answer:
**Andreas** (by Cynewulf) recounts the missionary journey of Saint Andrew (a saintly apostle) to Mermedonia, where he is captured by cannibals and subjected to prolonged physical torture over multiple days: bound in fetters, repeatedly scourged with blows causing sweat and blood to well from wounds, dragged across streets breaking his "bone-house," stabbed with "wound-gashes," and left in a prison amid winter storms, yet miraculously enduring without death.[^1]

**Juliana** (by Cynewulf) narrates the martyrdom of Saint Juliana of Nicomedia, who resists a pagan suitor and is tortured by her father and judge: stripped and scourged with whips, hung by her hair from a beam and beaten for hours, immersed in boiling lead and fire (killing her tormentors but sparing her), before beheading.[^2]

No other works in the corpus depict a saintly figure undergoing physical torture. Mentions of "saint," "martyr," "torture," "scourge," etc., elsewhere are metaphorical (e.g., poetic lovers as "martyrs"), historical/legal discussions (e.g., slavery or trials), or hellish punishments of sinners (not saints).

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [Chapter 1](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-655ebf), esp. paragraphs [p-655ebf](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-655ebf), [p-5d78fc](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-5d78fc), [p-17921e](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-17921e), [p-34b202](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-34b202), [p-dbf56f](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-dbf56f).

[^2]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, [Chapter 1](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-3fccbd), esp. paragraphs [p-3fccbd](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-3fccbd), [p-7cffd3](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-7cffd3), [p-89a6c9](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-89a6c9), [p-3a01e6](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-3a01e6).
